selected_windows.text: In this chapter we will go over the outcomes of the research activities described in the methodological section. Each sub-question will be addressed using the AWS services, we will make use of benchmarks, observation, and evaluations. The results will be shown using tables, images, screenshots, descriptive text, and coding where it’s needed. The screenshots mentioned in the text are show as (e.g. “as shown in figure”), you are able to right click on the text that says “figure”, and you will be taken to the figure itself in the appendix. This structure will be used to eventually answer the main research question.

To meet these methods, I have chosen two service categories to test this on: • EC2, is for traditional batch processing. To establish a fair representation of the two services, I decided to create three datasets. These datasets contain small, medium, and large amount of data, to see which service can handle the most amount of data, and to see which service can process the quickest. The small dataset is created by repeating the same words, this is done to show the number of times certain words are appearing in the dataset using the WFA.

Lastly, I ran both the medium and large dataset to check for not only the word counts but also the processing time. As you can see in the image the word counts are shown, apart from the large dataset, this is not only due to the usage of my free trial, but also due to the lack of sufficient RAM (1GB). Despite this error, it shows the limitations of running large text analysis tasks on low resource virtual machines. This demonstrates that EC2 is sufficient for small datasets, but that larger datasets require scalable computing services, to avoid memory bottlenecks. To show the outputs of the medium and large dataset I have implemented a screenshot of the out put as shown in Figure 7. 4 1.4 Lambda 1.4.1 Services Configuration To coordinate the processing pipeline, I designed the following UML diagram. To see the UML component diagram see Figure 8, Lamda UML component .

Then I inserted the role we created LambdaS3ReadWFA. • General configuration o I set an initial Memory of 512MB and a timeout of 60s. I updated this every time the dataset grew, up to 2048MB and a timeout of 300s. This ensures we had enough CPU and I/O bandwidth to complete the job without premature termination. To check the WFA, we need to create a piece of code that executes the WFA.

The most common word in this small dataset was “hello” . We can conclude that lambda handles trivial workloads immediately, with minimal resources. This demonstrates that the streaming approach avoids memory errors and delivers word-counting events even on 100MB files, this is seen in Figure 13. Last but not least, we did the test on the large dataset. You see that the execution time has significantly increased, and the max memory used also. The most common used words are still given and the a amount of unique words, results are shown in Figure 14. To conclude AWS lambda with streaming is suited for small WFA analysis tasks. It completes small tasks around 100MB within seconds, and this with only using the free tier. Even the bigger WFA of 500MB is succeeding in contrary to EC2. However, something you notice is that when the WFA become bigger, the execution time becomes drastically bigger. On a local set-up as mine, it already takes quite some time, for just 500MB. 1.5 Performance table After doing test on both EC2 and Lambda I will put them against each other to see which one is performing the best. Metric EC2 small EC2 medium EC2 large Lambda small Lambda medium Lambda large Execution time (S) 0.2 4.1 N/A 0.063 7.4 28.6 Memory used (MB) ~200 ~850 N/A 35 500 1800 Status success success failure success success success 1.6 Non-functional qualities Apart from the metrics used there are some critical non-functional factors that we need to consider. The EC2 requires you to do a manual provision, SSH set-up, and cleanup, this can increase the operational overhead by quite a lot.

But running the Lambda word frequency analysis on your local setup can be time consuming, since the execution time increases drastically when the file content increases. Amazon EC2, as we can conclude is also suitable for smaller datasets But when you are scaling up the content, it will start to struggle with resource limits and manual configuration limits. Also, since I used the free version, the performance plateaued due to memory limitations of the t2.micro instance. This shows that scaling for larger instance types of heavy workload is needed.
